food_subgroup: Mixed rice dish with fish — dum-style biryani

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani food product class; no fish-specific dum biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers biryani rice dishes broadly; no fish dum biryani-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for fish dum biryani or biryani dish found in published SNOMED CT release.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of fish dum biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with fish and served with raita or curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition); supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD fish biryani reference and USDA SR Legacy basmati rice and fish components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry for 'fish dum biryani' as a composite dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fish biryani reference (closest match), cross-checked against IMR Malaysia MyFCD biryani entries and USDA SR Legacy component data for basmati rice (USDA FDC ID 169704) and cooked fish. Weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish estimated at approximately 60% rice, 25% fish, 15% masala/fat/vegetables by cooked weight. Values represent a moderate-fat, moderate-protein mixed rice dish consistent with published South Asian biryani data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; basmati rice-based mixed dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fish dum biryani found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 58 based on the closest analogous food: basmati rice-based mixed dish (GI ~58, medium category) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dum cooking method and fat/protein content from fish and yogurt are expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice (GI ~50–58). GI 58 assigned as a conservative medium estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (58 × 21.5 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 43.9 g per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving contributes approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute a substantial fraction of the daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per standard serving (GL ~44); carbohydrate content of 21.5 g per 100 g from basmati rice is the primary glycaemic driver.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to approximately 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or raita to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response given the medium GI and high per-serving glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g) from ghee and yogurt, combined with high sodium per serving; fish component provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose preparations using minimal ghee and lean fish varieties, as the omega-3 content from fish is cardioprotective but the sodium and saturated fat from ghee-heavy preparations can offset this benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Dum Biryani is a fragrant, layered rice dish of South Asian origin widely served in Malaysia, Singapore, and the broader South Asian diaspora. It is prepared using the dum cooking technique, in which marinated fish — typically mackerel, pomfret, or snapper — is layered with parboiled basmati rice, caramelised onions, tomatoes, yogurt, ghee, and a complex blend of whole and ground spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, turmeric, chilli, and cumin. The assembled pot is sealed tightly and cooked over low heat, allowing the steam to infuse the rice with the aromatic fish masala. The result is a moist, intensely spiced rice dish with tender fish pieces, a golden saffron-tinted top layer, and a rich, layered flavour profile. Texturally, the rice grains are separate and fluffy while the fish is soft and well-seasoned. Nutritionally, fish dum biryani provides moderate energy (approximately 165 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from fish (8.5 g per 100 g), and complex carbohydrates from basmati rice. The fish component contributes vitamin B12, niacin, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. Sodium content is elevated due to spice blends and cooking salt, making portion control relevant for individuals with hypertension. The medium glycemic index (~58) and high per-serving glycemic load require attention for people managing Type 2 Diabetes. In Malaysian and Singaporean food culture, fish biryani is a popular Friday lunch dish, reflecting the Islamic tradition of consuming fish on Fridays, and is commonly sold at Indian-Muslim (Mamak) restaurants and hawker stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish biryani?
Is fish biryani high in sodium?
What fish is used in dum biryani?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry for fish dum biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fish biryani reference and cross-checked with IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice-based mixed dish data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted/estimated nature of both nutrient and GI values.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Friday - Fish Dum Briyani' has been canonicalised to 'Fish Dum Biryani'; the 'Friday' prefix is a menu label reflecting the Islamic Friday fish tradition and is preserved in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant preparation would be required for high confidence. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN fish biryani reference or the IMR MyFCD biryani entries used. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted sources. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted sources. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in traditional ghee-based preparations per ICMR-NIN data. 7. The cuisine_tags 'Noodle' and 'Dosa' from the input seed record are not applicable to this dish and have been discarded as erroneous seed metadata. 8. GI of 58 is a medium-category analogous estimate based on basmati rice mixed dish data; direct GI measurement for fish dum biryani is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g) / 10000 = (58 × 21.5 × 350) / 10000 = 43.89, rounded to 43.9.
